summary: This article analyzes how method-of-use patents, in conjunction with DSHEA preclusion and FDA exclusivity, create extended monopolies on public-domain compounds. It provides a strong theoretical framework and compelling case studies, arguing for preemptive prior art publication as a defense. The analysis is well-structured and addresses a significant issue in pharmaceutical access.
strengths: Clear and logical explanation of a complex regulatory and patent interaction.
Well-chosen and effectively presented case studies (colchicine, NAC, aptamers) that illustrate the core argument.
Proposes a practical, albeit challenging, solution (defensive disclosure) that is directly actionable.
Strong theoretical foundation supported by relevant legal and regulatory references.
weaknesses: The novelty of the core argument (using prior art to invalidate patents) is not entirely new, though its specific application to this 'squeeze' mechanism is novel.
The practical implementation of 'preemptive prior art publication' for every potential therapeutic use could be resource-intensive and face challenges in meeting enablement standards.
While the NAC case study highlights regulatory uncertainty, its direct link to patent amplification is less pronounced than the colchicine example.
suggestions: Elaborate further on the challenges and best practices for meeting the 'enablement standard' for defensive disclosures in pharmacological contexts.
Quantify the potential impact of widespread defensive disclosure on drug pricing and innovation, perhaps through modeling or further economic analysis.
Consider discussing the role of patent thickets and evergreening in exacerbating this 'squeeze' beyond just method-of-use patents.

claims.text: When a pharmaceutical company files a method-of-use patent on a new therapeutic application of a compound already sold over-the-counter as a dietary supplement, the patent interacts with the DSHEA drug preclusion clause (FD&C Act § 201(ff)(3)(B)) and FDA exclusivity grants to create a compounding restriction on public access.

In 2009, URL Pharma conducted the FDA-required randomized controlled trial for colchicine in acute gout. The FDA granted 3-year marketing exclusivity. URL Pharma also obtained method-of-use patents on specific dosing regimens (US7820681B1, US8927607B1).
With both exclusivity and patents in place, URL Pharma (later acquired by Takeda) raised the price from $0.09 to $4.85 per pill — a 5,300% increase.
NAC (CAS 616-91-1) was approved as a prescription drug in 1963 for use as a mucolytic agent and acetaminophen overdose treatment.

novelty_vs_existing.title: Preemptive Prior Art Publication as a Defense Against Method-of-Use Patents on Known Compounds and Methods
novelty_vs_existing.contribution: This submission provides a more detailed analysis of the interaction between DSHEA, FDA exclusivity, and method-of-use patents, and includes additional case studies (NAC, aptamers) that were not covered in the prior work. It also elaborates on the specific mechanism of the 'squeeze'.
